task: I’m getting my tires rotated soon and want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything basic. Can you help me figure out what I should check before I drive off, and whether there’s anything specific I should ask them to verify while it’s on the lift?

turns.agent_response:
Yep — for a tire rotation, I’d keep it simple and make them verify the stuff that actually matters.

### Before you leave
- **Lug nuts torqued properly**  
  Ask if they used a torque wrench, not just an impact.
- **Tire pressures set correctly**  
  Check all four tires, and the spare if you have one.
- **TPMS light off**  
  If your car has tire pressure monitoring, make sure the warning isn’t on.
- **Wheel covers / center caps seated**  
  Easy to miss after a rotation.
- **No vibration, wobble, or pulling on the test drive**  
  If they can do a quick road test, that’s worth it.
- **Final visual check**  
  Look for a missing lug nut, damaged valve stem, or anything obviously out of place before you roll out.

### While it’s on the lift, ask them to verify:
- **Tread wear pattern**
  - Uneven wear can point to alignment, inflation, or suspension issues.
- **Sidewall damage, bubbles, or cuts**
  - Especially on the tires that were moved from the rear to the front.
- **Brake condition**
  - Since the wheels are already off, it’s a good time to glance at pads, rotors, and leaks.
- **Suspension/steering obvious play**
  - Ball joints, tie rods, bushings, torn boots, anything visibly loose.
- **Wheel condition**
  - Bent rim, corrosion on the bead, damaged valve stems.

### Good questions to ask
- “Did you torque the lugs to spec?”
- “What PSI did you set them to?”
- “Did you notice any uneven wear or damage?”
- “Any signs of brake or suspension issues while the wheels were off?”

### Quick rule
If they rotated the tires but **didn’t check pressures and torque**, that’s not really a complete job.

If you want, I can give you a **30-second post-rotation checklist** you can use in the parking lot before you drive away.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much. But I’d want one more thing in writing or at least clearly said: **which tire went where**. I like to know the rotation pattern they used, because if one tire already had odd wear, I want to track it.

And I’d ask them to check:
- **spare tire pressure** too, if it’s full-size or accessible
- **lug nut condition** — not just torque, but whether any studs/nuts look stretched or rounded
- **TPMS relearn/reset** if the car needs it
- **alignment clues** if they see feathering or one shoulder wearing faster

I’ve had the whole “looks fine” thing bite me before. One small spec issue and you’re back in the bay wasting time. So yeah, the basics you listed are right, but I’d also want them to note anything weird they see instead of just rotating and handing me the keys.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — that’s the right add-on list.

I’d want **the rotation pattern documented** too, even if it’s just a quick note on the invoice or a verbal “LF to LR, etc.” That way you can track whether a tire’s wear follows a position or stays with the tire.

### I’d ask them to confirm:
- **Which tire went where**  
  Ideally written down.
- **Spare tire pressure**  
  If it’s a full-size spare or easy to access.
- **Lug nut / stud condition**  
  Not just torque — look for stretched studs, rounded nuts, cross-threading, or corrosion.
- **TPMS relearn/reset**  
  If your car requires it, make sure they did it or told you why not.
- **Alignment clues**  
  Feathering, cupping, inside/outside shoulder wear, or one tire wearing faster than the others.

### Good “while it’s on the lift” ask
> “Can you note the rotation pattern, check for uneven wear, and tell me if you see anything unusual with the lugs, studs, or suspension?”

That keeps it from being a blind rotate-and-go job.

### If you want the short version to use at the counter
> “Please rotate them, set pressures, and note which tire goes where. Also check the spare pressure if applicable, inspect the lugs/studs, reset TPMS if needed, and tell me if you see any uneven wear or alignment clues.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **tiny one-screen checklist** you can keep on your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically the list I wanted. The written rotation pattern is the part I’d care about most, and you included the extra checks I’d ask for.

That short counter script is usable too. No notes from me.
